did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a bio-inspired feed-forward system for skin lesion analysis. Specifically, it employs State-Controlled Cellular Neural Networks (SC-CNNs) for pre-processing and segmentation of dermoscopy images, and a pre-trained Levenberg-Marquardt Feed-Forward Neural Network for classifying skin lesions as benign or malignant (melanoma) based on hand-crafted features. The study was validated using the PH2 dermoscopy image dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original bio-inspired feed-forward system for skin lesion analysis, detailing a specific methodology involving SC-CNNs for pre-processing and a Levenberg-Marquardt Neural Network for classification. It describes the development of hand-crafted features, the training and validation of the proposed algorithm on the PH2 dataset, and reports experimental results, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Bio-Inspired Feed-Forward System for Skin Lesion Analysis, Screening and Follow-Up
brief_summary: This study proposes a bio-inspired feed-forward system for the automatic analysis and screening of skin lesions to discriminate between benign nevi and malignant melanoma. The pipeline utilizes State-Controlled Cellular Neural Networks (SC-CNNs) for pre-processing and segmentation of dermoscopy images, followed by the extraction of 21 hand-crafted morphological features. These features are classified using a pre-trained Levenberg-Marquardt Feed-Forward Neural Network with a 21-25-2 architecture. The method was validated on the PH2 dermoscopy image dataset, comparing performance against existing benchmarks using sensitivity, specificity, and a cost function. The proposed system achieved a sensitivity of 97% and a specificity of 95%, demonstrating superior performance compared to other methods in the ADDI project benchmarks. Additionally, the system provides a numerical array for follow-up rate assessment based on heuristic thresholds derived from the extracted features.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study classifies skin lesions as either benign nevi or malignant melanoma. The PH2 dataset contains 40 melanoma images. The system aims to discriminate benign lesions from malignant ones, with a specific focus on early detection of melanoma.

The study classifies skin lesions as either benign nevi or malignant melanoma. The PH2 dataset contains 125 benign nevus images (including common and dysplastic nevi). The system outputs [1, 0] for benign nevus classification.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into 'benign nevus' or 'melanoma' (malignant) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper uses a pre-trained Levenberg-Marquardt Feed-Forward Neural Network to classify skin lesions based on hand-crafted features. The output is a binary classification: [1, 0] for benign nevus and [0, 1] for melanoma. The system also provides a follow-up rate based on heuristic thresholds derived from these features, but the core AI task is discrete class prediction.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Database
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopy image dataset provided within the ADDI project, containing 176 dermoscopy images with segmented masks. It includes images of benign nevi (common and dysplastic) and melanoma.

use.training: Partially used for training the Levenberg-Marquardt Neural Network (along with images from other medical databases).
use.validation: Used for validation of the proposed pipeline and comparison with ADDI project benchmarks.
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed pipeline and comparison with ADDI project benchmarks.
use.other: Used as a reference for segmentation masks and ground truth labels.

datasets_used.name: Other Medical Database
datasets_used.description: A private dataset of dermoscopy images provided by oncologists, used in conjunction with the PH2 database.

use.training: Partially used for training the Levenberg-Marquardt Neural Network to introduce selected dermoscopy images with different patterns.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Training the Feed-Forward Neural Network to classify skin lesions based on hand-crafted features.
Used as a stop criteria for the neural network training.
To evaluate and compare the performance of the proposed system against existing benchmarks (ADDI project) for discriminating benign nevi from malignant melanoma.
To evaluate the performance of the proposed system.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, and a cost function, comparing results against existing benchmarks without statistical significance testing.

PH2 Database.notes: The proposed method achieved superior performance compared to existing benchmarks from the ADDI project. The cost function C was computed using weights c10=1.5 and c01=1, where c10 represents the weight for false negatives (melanoma classified as benign) and c01 for false positives (benign classified as melanoma). The system also provided follow-up rate recommendations based on heuristic thresholds for features F12, F17, and F21.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on a carefully selected training set of dermoscopy images containing benign and malignant lesions with different morphological features (color, border, geometry, irregularities), partially from the PH2 database and partially from a private medical database provided by oncologists.

architecture_details.dimensions: 21-25-2 (Input layer: 21 neurons, Hidden layer: 25 neurons, Output layer: 2 neurons)

training_details.framework: MATLAB with Image and Neural Network toolboxes
training_details.data_preprocessing: Input features F1...F21 were normalized. Source images were resized to 768x576 pixels.

classification_or_output_head.output_format: Vector [1, 0] for benign nevus, [0, 1] for melanoma
classification_or_output_head.post_processing: Heuristic thresholds applied to specific features (F12, F17, F21) for follow-up rate assessment

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of RGB dermoscopy images to YCbCr format and extraction of the luminance component (Y)
Application of State-Controlled Cellular Neural Networks (SC-CNNs) for transient pre-processing, including contrast enhancement and noise reduction
Use of specific SC-CNN cloning templates (A, B, C, I) to increase gray-level contrast and reduce distortion from dermoscopy gel
Segmentation of the skin lesion using masks provided by the PH2 database
Resizing of source images to 768x576 pixels
Normalization of gray-level values of the input source image for SC-CNNs
Logarithmic rescaling of hand-crafted image features to compress their value ranges
conclusions: The proposed bio-inspired feed-forward system, utilizing State-Controlled Cellular Neural Networks (SC-CNNs) for pre-processing and a Levenberg-Marquardt Feed-Forward Neural Network for classification, effectively discriminates between benign nevi and malignant melanoma.

The system achieved a sensitivity of 97% and a specificity of 95% on the PH2 dermoscopy image dataset, demonstrating superior performance compared to existing benchmarks from the ADDI project (which ranged from 76% to 100% sensitivity and 71% to 89% specificity depending on the method).

The cost function for the proposed method was 0.0682, which was lower than all compared ADDI project benchmarks, indicating better overall classification performance considering the weighted costs of false negatives and false positives.
The system provides a numerical array of 21 hand-crafted morphological features that can be used to define and assess follow-up rates for skin lesions based on heuristic thresholds.
The pipeline is computationally efficient, analyzing a single nevus in approximately 2.5 seconds on a standard PC, and is portable to embedded platforms such as STM32.

The authors acknowledge that the current study focuses on benign nevi and melanoma, and future work is needed to extend the dataset to include 'suspected' nevi to improve clustering precision for ambiguous cases.
important_remarks: The study is limited to binary classification of benign nevi versus malignant melanoma; it explicitly acknowledges that future work is needed to include 'suspected' nevi to improve clustering precision for ambiguous cases.

The system relies on ground truth segmentation masks provided by the PH2 database for validation, which may introduce bias if the masks do not perfectly align with the SC-CNN segmentation or clinical reality.

The training data is a hybrid of the public PH2 database and a private medical database provided by oncologists; the specific size and composition of the private dataset are not reported, limiting reproducibility and assessment of data leakage risks.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance comparisons against ADDI benchmarks are based on point estimates of sensitivity, specificity, and cost function without statistical significance testing.

The cost function weights the false negative (missed melanoma) at 1.5 times the weight of the false positive, reflecting clinical priority but potentially skewing the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity compared to unweighted metrics.

The method uses hand-crafted features derived from the luminance component only, discarding color information which is often critical in dermoscopy analysis, potentially limiting generalizability to lesions where color is a primary diagnostic feature.

The follow-up rate recommendations are based on heuristic thresholds derived from training data averages and oncologist advice, rather than statistically derived risk probabilities, which may lack robustness across different populations.
The paper reports computational efficiency (2.5 seconds per image) and potential for embedded deployment (STM32), but does not provide evidence of clinical workflow integration or user study results.
